criteria: The agent has read Section 2 completely to understand the unified optimization problem (Eq. 1) that spans both parameter and architecture spaces, including the four constraints C1–C4.

The agent has read Section 2 to understand the bilevel optimization reformulation (Eq. 2), where the upper level optimizes merged parameters and the lower level searches for the optimal inference path.

The agent has read Section 2 to understand the Tchebycheff scalarization strategy (Eq. 3) used to decompose the multi-objective problem into N preference-weighted scalar subproblems, using preference vectors sampled from a Dirichlet distribution.

The agent has read Section 3 (Parameter-Level Optimization) to understand the DARE-Ties merging procedure, including computing residual vectors, Drop-and-Rescale operation, Ties Merging, and the final optimal parameter formula (Eq. 5).

The agent has read Section 3 (Architecture-Level Optimization) to understand the model-layer candidate pool construction (Eq. 6), including the K fine-tuned models and the parameter-level merged model indexed as m=K+1.
The agent has read Section 3 to understand the MDP formulation for inference path search, including state space (Eq. 7), action space (Eq. 8), and reward function (Eq. 9).

The agent has read Section 3 to understand the actor-critic method, including the Gaussian-parameterized stochastic policy, the Wolpertinger discretization strategy (Eq. 11), and the PPO-based network update objectives (Eqs. 12-14).

The agent has read Section 3 to understand the dimension alignment via statistical matching, including the MLP-based scaling matrix (Eq. 16) and the Deep CORAL loss formulation (Eqs. 42-44 in Appendix B.4).

The agent has read Section 3.1 and Appendix C.1 to understand all experiment configurations, including the three LLM backbones (LLAMA-2-7B, Qwen-2.5-1.5B, LLAMA-2-13B), the fine-tuned models used for each, and the GPU setup.

The agent has read Appendix B.3 (Algorithm 1) to understand the complete HM3 training procedure, including input initialization, trajectory collection, MLP alignment, reward computation, and network update stages.

The plan includes generating N preference weight vectors from a K-dimensional Dirichlet(1,...,1) distribution to uniformly cover the probability simplex, and using Tchebycheff scalarization to decompose the multi-objective problem into N subproblems.

The plan includes implementing the DARE-Ties parameter-level merging procedure: computing residual vectors, applying Drop-and-Rescale, applying Ties-Merging sign-consistent aggregation, and computing the final weighted merged parameters (Eq. 5).
The plan includes constructing the model-layer candidate pool for architecture-level search, consisting of all layers from the K fine-tuned models and the parameter-level merged model (index K+1).

The plan includes implementing the MDP components: state as a full trajectory encoded by a GRU, action as a (model, layer) pair, and the reward penalizing path length (Eq. 9) uniformly distributed across time steps.

The plan includes implementing the Wolpertinger policy discretization: generating a continuous proto-action from a Gaussian policy, finding M nearest-neighbor discrete actions, and selecting among them by Q-value with epsilon-greedy exploration (Eq. 11).

The plan includes implementing the PPO-based actor-critic training: clipped surrogate objective for the policy network (Eq. 12), GAE for advantage estimation, and value loss for the value network (Eq. 13), with entropy regularization (Eq. 14).

The plan includes implementing the MLP-based dimension alignment layer trained with Deep CORAL loss (Eq. 42) and optional mean alignment loss (Eq. 43), updated jointly within the actor-critic framework.
The plan includes the key hyperparameters: Max_iter=1000, discount factor γ=0.990, 70%/30% dataset split for RL inference and merged model evaluation.

The plan includes loading the three LLAMA-2-7B fine-tuned models: Llama-2-7B-Chat (translation), WizardMath-7B (math), and CodeLlama-7B (code), and evaluating on GLUE, WMT14/WMT16/IWSLT2017/XNLI, GSM8K/MathQA, and HumanEval/MBPP.
The plan includes loading the three Qwen-2.5-1.5B fine-tuned models: Qwen-2.5-1.5B-Instruct (translation), Qwen-2.5-Code-1.5B (code), and Qwen-2.5-Math-1.5B (math) for the Qwen experiment.
The plan includes merging ViT-B/32 and ViT-L/14 CLIP backbones across eight vision datasets: DTD, GTSRB, RESISC45, SUN397, SVHN, MNIST, Cars, EuroSAT using classification accuracy.
The plan includes all six baselines: fine-tuned models (per task), Task Arithmetic, Ties-Merging, DARE-Ties Merging, PCB Merging, Consensus Merging, and EA.

The plan includes ablation experiments comparing HM3 against HM3 w.o. architecture optimization and HM3 w.o. parameter optimization in both single-objective and multi-objective settings using HV metric.
The code for sampling N preference weight vectors from a Dirichlet(1,...,1) distribution has been implemented to uniformly cover the K-dimensional probability simplex.
The code for the Tchebycheff scalarization (Eq. 3) has been implemented, computing the ideal point z_k* for each objective and then solving the min-max subproblem for each preference vector.

The code for the parameter-level DARE-Ties merging has been implemented: computing task residuals delta_k = Theta_k - Theta_base, applying Drop-and-Rescale (delta_k^DR = delta_k / (1-p)), Ties-Merging sign-consistent aggregation, and computing the final weighted merged parameters per Eq. 5.

The code for constructing the model-layer candidate pool for each subproblem (Eq. 6) has been implemented, containing all (model_index, layer_index) pairs from K fine-tuned models and the parameter-level merged model (m=K+1).

The code for the MDP state representation has been implemented: the state S_t is a trajectory of (model_index, layer_index, layer_parameters) tuples encoded by learnable identity/position encoders (psi_m, psi_l, phi) and a GRU to produce a fixed-dimensional hidden vector h_t (Eq. 7).

The code for the action space has been implemented such that at each step t, the action A_t selects the next (model_index, layer_index) pair from the candidate pool (Eq. 8) subject to dimensional compatibility.

The code for the reward function (Eq. 9) has been implemented: R = sum_k lambda_k * f_k(Theta, h) - beta_1 * T, computed after the full inference path is generated and MLP alignment is performed, then uniformly distributed as R_t = R/T across all time steps.
The code for the Gaussian-parameterized stochastic policy network has been implemented, outputting a continuous proto-action from distribution N(mu_pi(S_t), diag(sigma_pi^2(S_t))).

The code for the Wolpertinger policy discretization (Eq. 11) has been implemented: finding the M nearest discrete actions to the proto-action using Euclidean distance, then selecting the action with the highest Q-value (with probability 1-epsilon) or a uniformly random neighbor (with probability epsilon).

The code for the PPO-based policy network update (Eq. 12) has been implemented using the clipped surrogate objective with the importance sampling ratio rho_t(mu) and estimated advantage A_hat_t via Generalized Advantage Estimation (GAE).
The code for the value network and its loss (Eq. 13) has been implemented, estimating the state-value function V_phi(S_t) and minimizing the squared difference against target returns.
The code for the combined training objective (Eq. 14) has been implemented: L(mu, phi) = L_CLIP(mu) + c1 * L_VF(phi) - c2 * H(pi_mu), with entropy regularization.

The code for the MLP-based dimension alignment layer (Eq. 16) has been implemented: W_{m,l} = MLP_mu(m, l, t), trained using Deep CORAL loss (Eq. 42) plus optional mean alignment loss (Eq. 43), updated jointly during actor-critic training.

The complete HM3 Algorithm 1 (Appendix B.3) has been implemented: outer loop over N preference vectors, inner RL training loop for Max_iter=1000 iterations with trajectory collection, advantage computation, policy/value/MLP network updates, and output of the optimal policy and inference path.

The code for loading all LLAMA-2-7B series models (Llama-2-7B base, Llama-2-7B-Chat, WizardMath-7B, CodeLlama-7B) and the Qwen-2.5-1.5B series models (base, Instruct, Code, Math variants) has been implemented.

The code for evaluating language models on all specified benchmarks has been implemented: GLUE (lm-evaluation-harness), WMT14/WMT16/IWSLT2017/XNLI (lm-evaluation-harness), GSM8K (flexible match), MathQA (accuracy), HumanEval (pass@100), MBPP (pass@1).
The code for loading ViT-B/32 and ViT-L/14 CLIP models and their eight task-specific fine-tuned counterparts (DTD, GTSRB, RESISC45, SUN397, SVHN, MNIST, Cars, EuroSAT) has been implemented.
The code for the multi-objective evaluation using hypervolume (HV) metric has been implemented, with normalization using a shared reference set and reference point set at (1.1, 1.1).
The code for all six baseline merging methods (Task Arithmetic, Ties-Merging, DARE-Ties Merging, PCB Merging, Consensus Merging, EA) has been implemented or integrated using the mergekit package.
The ablation variants HM3 w.o. architecture optimization and HM3 w.o. parameter optimization have been implemented for comparison in both single-objective and multi-objective settings.

The code for the HM3 architecture-level training has been executed successfully on LLAMA-2-7B language experiments without errors, using A6000 GPUs (48GB each), with discount factor γ=0.990 and Max_iter=1000.
The code for the HM3 architecture-level training has been executed successfully on Qwen-2.5-1.5B language experiments without errors, using four 3090 GPUs (24GB each).
The code for all language task baselines (Task Arithmetic, Ties, DARE-Ties, Consensus, PCB, EA) has been executed successfully on LLAMA-2-7B and Qwen-2.5-1.5B without errors.
The code for ViT-B/32 and ViT-L/14 model merging (HM3 and all baselines) has been executed successfully on the eight vision classification datasets without errors.
The multi-objective experiments generating N Pareto-optimal solutions (S1-S15) and computing the HV metric against the MOEA baseline have been executed successfully without errors.
The ablation study experiments (HM3, HM3 w.o. arch, HM3 w.o. para) in both single-objective and multi-objective settings have been executed successfully without errors.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 1: HM3 achieves the best scores across all language tasks for LLAMA-2-7B, specifically GLUE=51.04, WMT&IWST=41.68, XNLI=40.24, GSM8K=45.62, MathQA=28.08, HumanEval=43.62, MBPP=44.62.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: HM3 achieves the best overall performance for Qwen-2.5-1.5B, specifically GLUE=48.22, WMT&IWST=32.26, XNLI=41.73, GSM8K=28.05, MathQA=40.13, HumanEval=34.31, MBPP=51.80.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 3: HM3 achieves average accuracy of 73.83% on ViT-B/32 across eight vision datasets (SUN397=63.42, RESISC45=76.27, SVHN=82.11, GTSRB=73.11, DTD=54.60, MNIST=96.85, Cars=64.63, EuroSAT=79.66).

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 4: HM3 achieves average accuracy of 85.34% on ViT-L/14 across eight vision datasets (SVHN=90.02, GTSRB=85.17, EuroSAT=89.32), outperforming all baselines.

The execution outcomes match the multi-objective results in Section 3.3: all baseline solutions (S1–S15 from HM3) dominate every competing method, and HM3 achieves an HV of 1.8120, significantly exceeding MOEA's HV of 1.5111 (or 1.6824 vs 1.1329 in Appendix C.3).

The execution outcomes match the ablation results in Table 6: in the single-objective setting HM3 achieves Translation=44.68, Math=45.62, Code=43.62; in the multi-objective setting HM3 achieves HV=1.8120, outperforming HM3 w.o. arch (HV=1.6387) and HM3 w.o. para (HV=1.3506).

The execution outcomes match the LLAMA-2-13B results in Table 5: HM3 achieves the best average performance with GLUE=53.20, WMT&IWST=41.12, XNLI=45.92, GSM8K=46.82, MathQA=29.13, HumanEval=43.93, MBPP=33.29.

The RL convergence experiment has been executed and shows that the mean reward increases progressively over 1000 episodes, initially slow during the first 200 episodes (exploration phase), then accelerating after episode 200 with experience replay, converging to approximately 18 by episode 1000 (Figure 6).
